chunk_text: I think it's good for them too because they like the numbers too. Like when I come to the reevaluation day, like the measurement day, they're always like, what was the last number like? chunk_text: I think that this information is really good to use when you're starting to get ready to discharge someone. Because they can say, I get this a lot. “But I feel like I'm getting better. You can't just charge me out.” Like, “I feel like I'm getting better. It's not time yet.” And then you can say, “well with insurance, you need to be showing measurable improvements. And these are your numbers. You're consistently 10% for the last two or three weeks. It looks like you're starting to maybe plateau a little bit.” So you can use that as part of the conversation with family members and with patients as well.
comment: get ready to discharge, share to caregivers

chunk_text: I think that with any type of outcome measure that you're using or anything that you're measuring to show improvement and to track data, I always think that it's important that you share it with the patients and the family members. Because this is what you're submitting to insurance. This is what you're saying, you're making progress or you're not making progress. And I think that they need that information. And I think that it can be a powerful tool that you say you're getting better. I think that's a really good motivator. Say like it seems like you're getting about the same. Sometimes that can motivate people to do more and to do better because they want to keep coming to therapy. And I also think that especially with therapy, it's not forever.

I would say that there are guidelines based on insurance and authorization and Medicare and things like that. But I think that it's really important as a therapist that you do what's best for the patient and you always advocate for what the patient needs. And the insurance, it can definitely be a barrier for sure but it's our job as the clinician to provide the information to the insurance company and say, "This is what I'm doing. This is why I'm doing it." And see there's improvement. And that's why we need to continue. And I don't feel like that's the burden on the patient and the type of insurance that they chose. That's the burden on the therapist to be able to prove that what we're doing is effective and necessary for the patient. So it is a factor but I really feel like it's our responsibility as therapists to do what's best for the patient and use our outcome measures and provide evidence and that's our job.

let's say you have a patient who is really inattentive to their upper extremity, and let's say they're a person with a chronic stroke. And they're like, "I just use my right hand because it's easier. I'm not going to use the left. It just takes too long. I'm only here because my wife or my husband forced me to come be here because they tell me I need to use it more." So that person I might start with, "Well, you don't have to do everything with that other hand but let's try to use them together because I think that the more you use them together, the more efficient you're going to be." So that might be someone I might use the duration of bilateral limb use. That way then I can say, well, I could see that you're using both your hands together more, how is it going? Do you feel like it's more frustrating? Do you feel like it's easier? It seems like two weeks ago you were only using bilateral hand use 10% of the time but now you're using it 30% of the time. Do you feel like it's getting easier? And that's why you're using it more? And I would talk to them about that and why the numbers are getting better or why the numbers are getting worse, things like that.

Like that would be the goal. And then this would be part of your outcome measure. Like the patient would wear this, you would get the data from these charts and now you're using these charts as your outcome measure to then say, you're working towards your goal. So let's say the goal is 20. They start at 5%. Your goal is 20%. And you can say like showing an improvement average percentage, improved from 5% to 10%, like making gains towards goal of reaching 20%.

My only other thing would be sizing. Because a lot of times people have a debit as upper extremities. So are they going to be able to easily get it on and off? Things like that. And also you want something that they don't have to constantly take onand off that with then you're better able to monitor their performance.

Nine Hole Peg Test and then I have my norms of age and gender and your hand dominant. So I put that paper in front of the patient. I put it in front of the family member and I'll say, "This is where you are, this is what's normal. And this is what is average. And this is what the average range is." So I'll always give them a reference to go off of. And depending on how impaired they are, I might say you might never get to this average number but I want to try to get you maybe to within normal limits. So always going over that information with them at evaluation and that anytime I redo the measurements, especially at like a reevaluation, I would say, this is where we started. This is where you are today. But I also think that in every single session, you should be addressing kind of what type of progress are you making? What types of difficulty are you having? How can we move you to that next level? So there are the big assessments that you do that you submit to the insurance company for authorization or to create a new plan of care and things like that. But every single session you're reassessing the patient, their skills and their abilities to just continue moving on board.
comment: share, reevaluate using normative data by standardized assessment

chunk_text: if you're going to put someone to be independent with something that would be more likely to be a longterm goal. If you need assistance with it, it's more likely to be a short term goal. If it's a patient who you don't think is going to be independent and might still need help, then you might have a goal that's like max assist at two weeks and moderate assist at six weeks. Do you know what I mean? So it really just depends on the patient, what the goal is and how much assistance you're going to be making it for.

But what I will say is that depending on what I'm seeing informally during this session will determine when I do a reevaluation. I use standardized assessment tools and also functional reporting measures, things like that to determine whether or not if the intervention that I'm providing is effective.
comment: use standardized assessment tool and functional report measures

chunk_text: On the contrast, if I get someone who comes in and they're a chronic stroke and they have more dexterity, they have more movement, but they're just like I feel like I'm just not using it as much. Like it feels a little weaker, things like that. Those patients I'll typically see depending maybe sometimes for a little bit longer, there's more than I can do with them. I'll continue to just do the functional things with them. For those patients, those outcome measures are super, super important. Because they're going to give me some hard data based on whether or not I'm seeing an improvement in strength and dexterity and function. Whereas those patients that are much more dense hemiplegia, they're not doing a ton of it at baseline anyways, you know what I mean? They have a much harder time incorporating their upper extremity. It's a task.
comment: focusing functional task for chronic patients

chunk_text: So for something like hand washing, are these water proof? Are they going to be able to put them under the sink? Things like that would be really important because we would want them to use both their hands for these tasks but if they have to take it off to wash their hands, do the dishes, things like that, it wouldn't give you the best idea of how well they're using their upper extremities.

So I could use it in a general way. Like, "We talked last week that when you were home you were going to use your arm more to do X, Y, and Z. Do you feel like you made any progress then?" And then they would respond and then I could always pull this data and be like, "Well, you know what? It looks like that on Thursday you use your arm a lot. What types of things did you do? But on Tuesday it looks like you're a little bit below average than where we'd like you to be or what you were the other day. What did you do that day?" That we then they can kind of explain like, "Well, I was cooking for my family and that's why this day is so high but this day I was really tired. I wasn't feeling well. So that's why my numbers aren't as high as they should be." So I could use that kind of as just like feedback, getting more information, some self awareness for them like, "I did this that day. And my number is really low. I should have used my arm more." I would prompt them like, "Well, this is really low, but you were doing this, how could you have incorporated your arm?" And then they could give me some ideas and some strategies, things like that. So more of like a self awareness tool. It holds them accountable too when there's a number, the numbers don't lie. And we know when our patients are doing our exercises and we know when they're not and they'll tell you up and down that they're doing them but sometimes you know that they're not. So this is holding them accountable.

So I like that it includes the bilateral hand skills because for some patients we're are working a lot just on the single arm and sometimes we'll constrain the good arm and just have them work the weaker arm so that they can improve. But for some patients, really the bimanual stuff is really important especially if they're inattentive. Don't just use the single hand, make sure you're using the other hand together. And sometimes that's an easier place to start than just forcing them to use the side that they aren't really super aware of. So I like that you have this component too, in addition to just the single arm use. I think that this information can be really beneficial as well depending on the type of client that you have and the type of issue that they're having.

So I might say patient will be independent with adaptive devices for self feeding or something like that. I can give you an example since you're trying to implement a device that's going to track data, something that let's say I was using this device and I wanted to provide a measurable goal. I could say like patient will increase use of affected upper extremity by 10% in spontaneous activities of daily living as evidenced by whatever, like the 10% improvement with this device.

So I think it's really important that as much information that you have and the lens that you're seeing it through as a therapist, I think it's really important that you convey that to the patient so that they can understand your problem solving and your thought process especially when it comes to continuing with therapy or stopping therapy, things like that. And coming up with a plan on how we're going to continue to progress things like that.

So I think that it depends on the patient, right? So if the patient has minimal dexterity, 50%, I don't know if that's a reasonable goal. I mean, I guess it depends on what you're using it for. It really depends on how much movement they have. And also if your left hand dominant and you have a lot of dexterity, the goal might be for more than 50%. You might be at 75%. So I really think it depends on the patient, their hand dominance and how much strength and coordination that they have. So it's going to be patient specific. I don't think that you can use that 50% for everyone because everyone's different.
comment: personalized goal setting with hand dominance, dexterity, data

chunk_text: So I think that you can use it to talk about their progress and how they're using it or like you can say, like I noticed in physical therapy during that hour, there's very little bilateral hand use. Can you make sure when they're doing their exercises, like you're incorporating their upper extremity. Some PTs are really good and some PTs are very good at it. And I mean, I'm sure they can say the same thing for OT about things that they're working on. So I think that you can always use that as a talking point, if you needed something concrete, usually just say it and they're happy to help you out. So I guess you could use the information or just saying. A lot of times what I do when was working in outpatient, I would pass the patient off to speech or to physical therapy, I would say, "Mister or missus so and so did really well today. They're making really nice improvements with X, Y, and Z." So I might use this information and say, "They're doing really well with their arm. On Tuesday they used it 50% of the time. And like, we've been working really hard on this." So like I do sometimes when I pass patients off, I just try to say something nice about them as I pass them off so that they can feel really encouraged. And then that gives them something to talk about with the other therapist.

So I think that you had it mentioned that it's not super specific. It's just like driving home. It's not talking about specific activities. So what I might do would be like let's say home, I saw a really high spike in home at 12:00 PM. I might be like, well, what were you doing during this time? And then I can say like it looks like you used your arm really, really well when you were preparing that meal, how did it feel? Was it difficult? But then I might see like home are really low, people cook. What were you doing at this time? Which I kind of thought that we had touched on this briefly. So I feel like that's why I would use the information to use it as a talking point. I mean, I don't really know how else you can be more specific than kind of what you have here. And I think that it's kind of just like an opener to build discussion about kind of how they're functioning at home and in the community.
comment: share with patient overally, identify task, interview with data

chunk_text: So I used to work for a facility that had a very large neuro based population and we would do kind of like a mini, like neuro rounds, like every couple of weeks where it would be PT, OT speech. And we would all sit in the same room when we would talk about all of our patients, just making sure that we're carrying over strategies from discipline to discipline. In terms of like OT to OT or OT to PT, we're always trying to educate each other and just trying to reinforce like what we're doing in our sessions. Even if it's not formal, it might be informal. And then from OT to OT, I'm sharing a patient with someone else, not only am I writing my note but if there's something specific, I would relay it to the other OT. So I would say that I would say pretty frequently we are discussing a patient status with colleagues in disciplinary but also interdisciplinary as well.

Constraint-induced Movement, have you guys heard about this before? (Yes.) So for something like that, the duration of bilateral limb use, that's not going to be beneficial for that type of client because we're restraining the other arm. You're not going to get them... That information's not going to be relevant. But if we're talking about the ratio of how often they're using it when you're doing the constraints, you can see whether or not they're cheating. Because if you see as a similar ratio, then you know that they're not constraining the other arms. So that would be a really important piece of information to have. And then also the duration. Are they using it as often as they're supposed to while the other limb is supposed to be restrained or constrained. So that's an example of why one of these would work better for a certain patient but not work better for another patient.
comment: using ratio measure for constrained-induced therapy

chunk_text: So if I were an outpatient, it's a laptop, it's like Office, like a Microsoft type tablet. So I would assume that... I don't know how you guys are planning on storing the data where it would be a website and you would log in and you would track it that way. Or if it's going to be an application you have to log in. I don't know how the data would be stored. I don't know if it's web based. That seems probably easier. So that's how I would access the data.

So in regards to the typical practices of an outpatient therapy setting mostly for OT, what we're going to be looking at the most is function. So how well are they able to do their everyday tasks, like more functional movements? Can they open things, close things, manipulate objects. So that would be the biggest thing that we're working on in an outpatient therapy session. And then in terms of motor learning, what we're looking at is we want to do lots of repetition, a lot of practice, a lot of repetitive things over and over again and just to enforce the motor learning.
comment: functional movement, motor learning is important

chunk_text: So informally, it would just be talking to the patient, observing how they're doing with the task in therapy. And then we also have our standardized tools that we would use. And then we also have our functional reporting outcome measures which are more like survey based things that the patient would self rate kind of how they're doing with different tasks.

So let's say I have a patient who comes in and they have dense hemiplegia, really not a ton of function prior. Sometimes those patients will come in and they'll complain of increased pain, increased stiffness, more difficulty lifting and manipulating the arm when they're doing their ADL. They might not actually be incorporating that arm for any ADLs. Maybe they're just like holding something but they can't manipulate objects, things like that. So that I would do more of patient education, go over stretching with the patients, stretching with the family members, a lot more education based things because they don't have a ton of function in the arm. We reiterate how to use it as a dependent stabilizer, things like that. But that's going to be more of my focus and those patients based on how their pain is, they're usually less sessions because there's less kind of things that I can do with them. It's more just maintenance, giving a refresher and a lot of education so that those symptoms don't get worse again in the future.

I think based upon the patient, I think for talking even just like habits and routines, I think if you ask a patient to wear it one day a month or one day a week for six months, that's going to be hard for people to remember. I think especially for chronic stroke, a lot of them are really discouraged. They're like, I had this stroke however many years ago. A lot of them have had doctors told them they're never going to get better. A lot of them have had people just say like, "That's how it is," not really encouraging them a whole lot to keep going, to continue to have hope, to keep trying to use their affected arm. I think even just changing people's mindset from a, "Well, it is what it is. I can't change anything. I'm stuck here," to a more empowered mindset of, "Okay, I may not see necessarily a huge gain from today to tomorrow, but if I'm consistent and if I keep trying to use my arm, maybe in the long run, I can get better at this. I can achieve progress. It may be more of a marathon versus a sprint analogy, but like I could make progress." Finding ways to even just get that patient buy in to start doing a little bit more every day, I think that's huge because it is definitely a challenge, especially when doctors have told patients like, "Well, it is what it is." People don't want to do any more exercise after that. Because if you've been told this is how it is, that takes away any kind of motivation to keep going and keep trying. I think it's really important that patients see that even over time that even if it is just a couple percentage points average just across the day, I think even that would represent a lot to many of the patients that do have the chronic stroke. I really think that could be empowering to them to say, "Oh, I made a change. I did something more with my arm. Wow, Maybe I can do this."

I think research very much supports that the more modalities or different methods, I guess I should say, of learning that you can incorporate, the better because some people are visual, some people are tactile, some people are hands on or kinesthetic learners. The more information, the more multisensory way you can convey your information to people, whether that's electronically, paper form, verbally, the more senses you can incorporate, the more likely you are to actually effectively communicate with people. I think that the more you can do that to even incorporate different learning styles, even with cognitive deficits, I think that that's very important.

so having a graph where it's like, "I know that you feel like you're using your arm all the time, but based upon this sensor data, it says you're not really moving at all that much. The biggest concern I would have like with the bulky sensors as opposed to the sleek ones is, I think if patients already have aphasia of motor planning difficulties, etcetera, having something like the higher profile ones with, it's going to be more likely to get caught on clothes and/or maybe cause potential like skin tears or something. Because if people are truly aware of where their arms are in space and they're moving about, I feel like already sometimes if they do maybe have left neglect or something like that, for example, they already aren't super aware of their hand and they might accidentally whack it on a doorframe or something like that.

Then maybe during your session, you go over like specific activities to their life, that they could incorporate into their just daily activities and stuff to help them achieve those goals. Is this too hard? Do they have pain while they do this?" I don't think it's ever good to just be like, "Here, do this." chunk_text: But in general, I like ... I mean, for patients who are motivated, I can see this being a good extra motivational tool.

They feel like they don't have time to sit and use their affected arm. So, it could be useful in that way where, say for example, at 11 o'clock, or 9 o'clock James was dressing himself, and not using the arm very much because he was in a rush. Patients doing their daily activities. They can come in and tell me as much they want that they're using their affected arm, but a lot of times I'm not seeing progress, or in the clinic I'm not seeing that.

say James were to come to me tomorrow, and I could look at this data and review it with him, I would probably ... Well, I guess two things. so one of the challenges that I was thinking of would be patients who have the ring but aren't very motivated. Or, one day they wake up and they don't feel like they want to wear the ring on their affected hand, but they don't want to hear form their therapist that they should be using it more.

So, we use box and blocks, and the nine hole peg test, if they're able to do those things. Range of motion, manual muscle testing. And typically, we also do some sort of subjective measure, like the upper extremities functional index, or sometimes I'll use the quick dash, depending on how much use of the arm they have. We also do grip strength and pinch strength if appropriate.

The objective part would be the standardized assessment tools. So, any scores on the box and blocks, the nine hole peg test. We've done the Minnesota manual dexterity test. And then grip and pinch strength. Like, all of those with the more concrete numbers. If someone doesn't have enough function to do those assessments, I'll often do a range of motion, either active or passive range of motion goal. But range of motion is not quite as standardized as some of these other tools. They've found a lot of flaws in how people measure range of motion. So, I try to reserve that for patients that really can't do anything else.
comment: limitation of standardized assessment for severe patients and limited accuracy of range of motion

chunk_text: The one thing actually that it could fall into the assessment category, but I think it's important each therapy session to get an update from the patient, like how they feel like they're progressing, or any new challenges that they're having. Other than that it looks good.

The one thing that I mentioned earlier on, that it's not something to measure, but if there's a way to figure out how to cheat proof the device, so that a patient can't just put the ring on their child, or just like take their unaffected arm and move their unaffected arm around. There has to be some accountability from the patient. We can't expect the device to do everything.

The other thing I would do is ask him, "What were you doing at 11 o'clock?" And problem solve through whatever activity it was and see why he was only at 10% at that time. Like, what was specific to that activity that he, for whatever reason, didn't use the affected arm as much. Is it something else we can figure out, like a role for the affected arm. Or was it that he just wasn't paying attention, and just not thinking about how he needed to use the affected arm. So, digging deeper into what specific activities he was doing, and seeing if in the clinic we can find a way for him to integrate the affected arm more.

chunk_text: But yeah, if they were able to go in, in the evening, and see what their daily score was. Like, kind of like a Fit Bit. And then, let me fill in some of these blanks. there's really no other way for me to know except for what they tell me or sometimes what their spouse or whoever the caregiver is, what they tell me. chunk_text: Time constraints, mostly.

To be able to distinguish between what activities they're doing. Like, I'm trying to think of an example. So, opening a jar, for example. chunk_text: We definitely do it at their first session, and then either every 30 days ... So, based on Medicare requirements, we have to reassess every 30 days, or 10 visits. we really just have to rely on what they tell us and hope that they're being honest when they tell us how much they're using their affected arm outside of therapy sessions. chunk_text: Yes, it's challenging. The goals become much more function based. So, I really can't include anything standardized. chunk_text: You could maybe do like by half hour. But if I ask him about a more specific time period, then maybe he'll have a better recollection. Like, "Oh, I eat a snack between 11 and 11:30." Versus, "Between 11 and 12 I eat a snack and I watch TV and I'm on the computer."

chunk_text: But, I think that if this were, not to continue bringing this up, but I think that if this were more of a time unit of measurement, that that might be more useful to patients to understand I used my hands together for three minutes at 10 am and one minute at 11 am. ... They could set a timer on their watch, or whatever, and do an activity for five minutes. And so, they know that the next hour, they're going to get more than they did the previous two hours. Or the next day.

depending on the severity of the cognitive impairment, I would probably just stick with the ratio piece of the measurement and try to say, "Yesterday you used your affected arm 20% of the time, and your unaffected arm 80% of the time. I really want you to try to use your affected arm more and let's see tomorrow if it can be closer to 30% and 70%. I think that that's probably the easiest way for someone with a cognitive impairment to grasp what you're asking of them. So, instead of breaking it down into bilateral, or breaking it down into the duration percentage just saying, "I'm not seeing that you're using your affected arm as much as your unaffected arm. I want you to try to use them both together more." And then just look at that ratio data with them, would be the way I would go about it.

Especially for, I can think of a few patients who tell me, "Oh, yeah. I'm using my affected arm at home all the time." But I think if they were to actually see more objective data like this, it would give them an idea that you're not actually using it as much as you think you are. So, try to use it more. Try to bring these numbers up. I feel like patients are most often, especially in stroke, patients are most often motivated by numbers. By concrete, objective data. Otherwise they kind of ... This is a generalization. But many patients I've had, they tend to not see their own progress unless it's concrete. Unless it's, "You increased by five pounds of grip strength." And then they kind of get excited about that. ... So, I could see with something like this that setting a goal to reach 40% and then being able to see that they've reached that goal 40%. It's an objective measure of them using their arm more, which is an important part of being able to use the arm more in the future.
I can see up to around 12 or 13. Not all of those patients have had strokes though. I think probably 80% of my caseload. And some days it's as low as four or five people. But at maximum, around 13.

chunk_text: "I cannot fix you. I cannot make you better. You have to have behavior change and get better." I mean some people do that but they're really, really serious about it. But generally, people just put some weight on, and they're like, "It felt good." That's what stroke recovery is like. They're like, "I don't know. I tried to do some stuff," but it's so poorly tracked. They have no idea if it's better or not. It's just my life.

But if you pick a short time frame, probably someone would do it. And then if they see that it's benefiting them, they might do it for longer. Really, I do have to go very, very soon but I could talk again if we want to talk more about this because this is fun.

Good question. For insurance-based visits, you are supposed to do some kind of reassessment monthly. It doesn't mean you have to do all of them; sort of no one is checking. I would say a best practice is to repeat something monthly. It doesn't mean that you have time your session to do all of those things, and you probably can't do all of those things in one session anyway. You're not going to do the Jebsen Hand Function Test, the Wolf Motor, and Box and Blocks. You're probably going to pick and choose. Does the patient have any hand function? Then maybe if they have very little, you might do Box and Blocks, record the number as zero and move on because they can't do it. That would be the easiest thing to do. If they do have hand function, then you pick a test that suits that person.

chunk_text:
I think some of the barrier comes from our training and education. OTs are trained to be generalists. When I get out OT school, I can work in pediatrics with kids with autism. I can work in a mental hospital. I could work with homeless population. I could work in ergonomics. I could work in neuro rehab. I can work in 100 different ways and still be an OT with the same education.

We learned specifics on the job. If you learned specifics in a skilled nursing facility, it's going to be extremely different from if you learned the specifics from a place that has great, great care. You're going to learn from the people that you work with. Are they going to be people who are trained 30 years ago and they're using antiquated techniques? That's what you will learn because, otherwise, just learned the basics. You learned how to not kill people in school, and then you learn how to actually treat on the job, and it matters who you work with.

You get paid better if you work in a nursing home or home health or something like that. You don't get paid as well if you work at [Hospitals] where I did. The nicer the facility, sort of the less you're going to get paid. Some people are just willing to go work in a nursing home and provide horrible therapy, and get paid $90,000. And I chose to do it a different way and worked somewhere that I believed in, and I got paid crap, but I love my job and I have a lot of knowledge.
comment: why therapist don't follow-up carefully -> lack of specialized education, paid better for worse facility

chunk_text: I would love to Fugl-Meyer. It’s terrible I would love to not use that anymore. It's a horrible assessment. It's just the gold standard, so it's what everybody does. But the purpose of the Fugl-Meyer is to understand where you are in terms of recovery. It's supposed to be used for the first month, but we use it for years. It's not used correctly, but it is the assessment that is used.

... skill level. Yeah. Using a test like Wolf Motor Function Test or the Jebsen provides you with more information about what the person can do but just, especially in hospital phase, so many people with stroke can't do those things, and that would just be demoralizing for them. So you stick to more of those, "Can you get a block from one side to the other?" You stick to very simple activities where you might see more change as opposed to stacking checkers or picking up the paperclip, things that they probably can't do.
comment: standardized tests rely on simple activity

chunk_text: I would say try something for three months. If you see your difference in three months, maybe you do it longer. If you see no change in three months, probably you're doing something wrong. But also, maybe they're not willing to make a change. I feel like you can't ask someone to sign-up for a year or something. I would be slow to be like, "I'll do this for a year. This is why diets don't work." We're like, "I'm never going to have me together. I'm never going to ... I don't know. Cut out dairy for my whole life," and you're like, "Probably won't." But if you'd say like, "I'm going to try something for..." that's why people do the whole 30 diet where they're going to eat no sugar, no carbs whatever for a month, people will do that for a month.
comment: 3 months or longer in longitudinal wearing

chunk_text: I would want to know what he was actually doing. In constraint-induced movement therapy also, they have this daily schedule that says, "What were you doing during these times?" They didn't have sensors but they would, without the sensor, try to figure out what you were up to.

I'm actually treating them in the clinic, I would look at the days. I would see because are they participating? It was really hard you have to do this breakup with people. If they are not changing over time and you're pretty sure they're not doing the recommendations you gave them, insurance will deny claims because you have to write this down in the note that says adherence is poor. Insurance is like, well, they're not going to spend $200 for you to see that person. Forget it. So if you have a way to monitor them that is effective, first, they should make changes because you help them understand that what they do at home is important.

chunk_text: If someone wants their insurance company to pay for their therapy, they should be using their arm more. If you want to utilize the resources of a therapist and the clinic, you should be putting in work. If you're not engaging in the therapy program, then you should not be using the therapy program. But that is a conversation that we have to have based off of our intuition and say, "It seems like you're not doing your home program," and they're like, "No, I swear I am," and you're like, "But you're not getting better and everything is the same or it's trending down." This is a really hard conversation that we have to have with people often. If I had data to show it that would say, "Look, there is no change and I'm providing you the best I know how. If you cannot follow this instruction, I cannot work with you." The only thing I have to go on right now is my intuition as to whether or not I think they're doing it. Sometimes, I'm probably wrong. Maybe they are trying. Maybe what I'm giving them is not correct or maybe they can't follow it, and there's something else needs to change but without data that says whether or not they are engaging at home, I might make the wrong choice and tell people they can't come to therapy anymore even though they are giving it their best shot.
comment: no progress -> insurance denial -> therapy termination, decision can be wrong with inaccurate observation, no follow-up data

chunk_text: if you do a high intensity protocol in the clinic and nothing else, nothing happens. You need something that tracks them afterwards so they can see what they're doing. Otherwise, people say great. It's like you go to the gym and you do all this exercise. You come home and have ice cream. You're like, "Well, I got those out of the way, and then I'm done." But if you're trying to have a weight loss program, you have to exercise and eat healthy. It's the same thing here. If you want to improve your arm function after stroke, you have to move and you have to change your activity patterns. You have to have both.
comment: high protocol in session is limited to make progress, importance of home tasks

chunk_text: If you wanted to add to it, you could have someone extrapolate what they did during that, like I'm home but what does that mean? Was I cooking? Was I laying on the couch? Do you have interface or a computer program or app? Do you have a way that they can add information?
If you're going to prescribe a home activity or a home task or a home routine, that’s going to change performance in the home.

chunk_text: In a single day, six or seven. We did one hour sessions, individual. It was a bit of a specialized clinic, so really high quality. I think that's the low end, and many therapists would see more than that. But I would see as many as seven hours in a row.

In regular therapy, there is no way for us to track adherence. What we get is patient interview, patient report, and then the wife is behind there going, "No, he doesn't use this arm at all." That's where we get accurate report is the caregiver is like, "They don't do it." It's known that people are going to overestimate how much they do. They're telling you they're using their arm 25%, it's probably 10%. To get a number that you can look at you feel confident in is huge, and then you have something to build off of. Otherwise, it's all patient report and there is no way to know if it's accurate or not.
comment: rely on patient's self-report or caregivers

chunk_text: It also gives the client this objective feedback that says, is what he is doing effective? Is that number changing? If it is trending down, what he is doing at home is not effective. If this is trending up, finally, something tells him he's doing it right because I can give someone a score and I can range a motion their arm and say, "Oh, look, you can reach higher," or, "Oh, your Fugl-Meyer is better." They do not believe you. They're like, "Nah, it feels the same to me," especially if that change is really slow. There's no way for them to understand what the change is. But objective feedback, you cannot argue with that. And they need objective feedback in OTs and PTs but OTs especially are known to not give objective feedback. We give, you're doing great, and that is patronizing, and they totally don't believe us. So objective feedback is necessary.
comment: objective feedback to patients, effective communication to patients

chunk_text:
I would say it should change. It should change. It should be a significant difference. Again, I'm a researcher now, so I have big dreams, and it's harder than that in the clinic. I get that. I mostly worked with chronic. We had people that would come to us straight from stroke, but then people would stay with the clinic for years and years and years, pay out of pocket if their insurance wouldn't pay for it.

I understand that change over time is small, but if the goal is 50% and they have a year to get there, they should get there. Why wouldn't they get there by then? This means to me that the therapy is not good enough, like what they're being told to do is not good enough. If they're not being told to do anything and they're just performing their life and using the data as sort of biofeedback and saying, "Oh, you're moving a little bit, great, good job," this is what I would expect. But if they're getting therapy on top of this that understands these principles that says high repetition and performance change or activity change, that number should change. It should go up. If they're getting status quo, you show up to the clinic, you put them on an arm bike, and tell them to do exercises at home, this is what it will look like. This is conventional therapy. Nothing really changes.

Again, if I'm looking at a client and they're working on the quality of their movement, not the number of repetitions, then I might not choose to use an accelerometer, I would use a different type of measure that's going to look more at the quality of their movements.
comment: quality of movement, measures are not for reporting quality of movement

chunk_text: I would worry about the compliance of the participants wearing a sensor every day for even a month, and certainly for a year. That's quite a long time.
comment: doubt in compliance of wearing sensor in long-term

chunk_text: I'll be honest, having gadgets, having new things like rings to put on your fingers that can sometimes be motivating too, like, "Oh, cool, I get to wear these new things, and I'm going to measure these things." So, that could be motivating as well.

I'm a big proponent of Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy as well. In fact, I've worked at [Hospital] at the end of its site trial, so this would be a fabulous way to compare, maybe get a baseline of amount of movement that they're using their arm before you institute Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy. And then, having them where the mit, not doing the full protocol like excite six hours a day, every day for two weeks, but being able to monitor how much they are using their affected arm and hand during times away from the therapist would be really, really useful.
comment: compare measure before and after constraint-induced therapy

chunk_text: If the person is plateauing and not showing improvement, the insurance company might say, "Well, they've gotten as good as they can get, we're not going to reimburse anymore." When in fact, maybe the person is working on the quality of their movement, not necessarily the repetition of the movement. Maybe they're working on getting that movement just right, rather than doing it really fast multiple times.
comment: no progress -> insurance termination, the measure not suitable for reporting quality of movement

chunk_text: In a single day, ideally they could wear it eight to 12 hours. If you can get them to wear eight to 12 hours reliably, that would be ideal. And then, I would like to see over a seven-day period, so you get an idea of, are there certain days that they're doing more, and certain days they're doing less. I think seven days would be a good range to be able to see that

It might be maybe for clients that are able to get out more, it might be interesting to see, are they at home, versus grocery store, versus place of worship, versus community center, versus driving. That would give you an idea of how much participation they're having within the community that might be... And, I think that would be less invasive. So, that might be a good measure of their participation in the community, and activity in the community.

it's disheartening. There's a very short amount of time that I've spent working in long-term care facilities. That was one of the reasons I didn't want to work there anymore because they want us to almost force patients to participate in therapy so that they could keep billing for therapy.
comment: long-term care facility sometimes force patients to participate therapy

chunk_text: Just personally in my own experience, and of course it's not wide ranging, I've seen probably more borderline abuse of that. Let's report favorable things, or report things that are going to get us more visits in a long-term care facilities, more so than I have in outpatients.

My first thought is showing clients that we're tracking your movements like this can be a motivator to them. To show them numbers and saying, "You're averaging only 22% movement in your affected arm and hand. Next week, I want you to get up to 30%." And, just having that as a motivator, having a goal like that I think can be really motivating to a client. I know just wearing Fitbits or something that lights up by vibrates when you reach a certain goal, clients like that. And, even without me having tell them some clients will wear a Fitbit, and they'll say, "If I didn't reach my step goal, I'll move my arms some more, so I get the vibration, or I get the lights up." So, I think it can be used as a huge motivator.
comment: motivation by achieving a goal, get alarmed to encourage

chunk_text: My rule of thumb is trying to find that just right challenge. So, encouraging them to do things that are not so hard that they're failing all the time, but really encouraging them to do activities that are maybe challenging somewhat, but that they can still succeed at. And finding ways, if possible to adapt the activities, not as an end to compensate and use the adaptation, but can we adapt it in certain ways, so that they can do it and keep working at it, and then slowly decreasing the adaptation. Something as simple as just the built up grip, or a spoon or fork. If they go out to eat, maybe they could still feed themselves using that built up grip that they could just slip on to a spoon or a fork, and then they could still feed themselves. And then, hopefully as they get better, you can reduce the size of that built up grip until it's smaller or down to nothing, just as an example of adaptive equipment.
comment: identifying task to encourage is challenging

chunk_text: And, I guess that's just the nature that I had always administered therapy. You see the person you suggest or give them home exercise programs, or home programs to do on their own, and then when they come back to see you, you follow up, you ask them how they did. What are their problems with, what did they do, what did they not do. Sometimes they tell you the truth, sometimes they wouldn't, and you could usually tell if they aren't telling you the truth. So, yeah.

Potentially for lower, very low level clients, or those that don't have any movement or very, very little movement, especially after a period of time, it just might not be feasible to work on continued use of that arm and hand, and so you rely on teaching them compensatory methods, adaptive methods, so that they can be safe and as independent as possible. So, if somebody is in that instance. If there's somebody that I'm only going to be able to see maybe two or three times, and I need to get them to be safe and independent to be able to go back home. I'm probably going to focus more on compensatory and teaching them hemi techniques to use the unaffected side, more so than focusing on use of the affected side. So in that instance, I don't think I would use it as much.
comment: sensor data will not be used to very severe patients, focusing compensatory movement

chunk_text: So, the standardized tools that I like to use for stroke severity or stroke patients would be the Motor Activity Log, to assess how much and how well they're using their affected arm and hand. The Fugl-Meyer Assessment to assess the impairment of movement. The Stroke Impact Scale to assess their level of participation in activities, and how the stroke has affected them as in health related quality of life measure. For research purposes, I frequently use the Wolf Motor Function Test. Clinically, though it's too long to administer. It's a great assessment for research, but just too long in a clinical setting to usually administer.

That makes sense. I think of the three measures, that would probably the one I would use least. And as a clinician, I'm thinking there's so many times where, I don't know if you can see me, a person will use their other hand to place it, and that's not always a functional bilateral use of it. So, I think I would wonder how much of that bilateral use was functional, and how much of it was adapted, or compensatory. I understand why you're doing it, and I think it's an interesting thing to look at, but I also don't know that it would tell the client that much. I'm thinking if I wanted to encourage bilateral movement, I would really have to stress and work with the client, meaning bilateral movement doing functional things, not lifting the arm up and placing it, or moving this arm with the other arm. Does that make sense?
comment: bilateral -> least used measure, how much bilateral use is functional?

chunk_text: The more you could see the better, but maybe if it's 14 days, I'd like to see every other day data. Again, I think the more you could see, trying to see as much as you can is possible. I think if I was seeing a client every other week, I'd still encourage them to wear it every day, so I could get it knowing that in reality they're going to forget some days, or they're just not going to do it some days. And so, I'll get as much as I possibly can.
comment: as much as they can wear,

chunk_text: The other problem I had using accelerometers on the wrist is I would be really specific, and I would mark them right wrist, left wrist, and they would still mix, switch them up, and that just messes up your data completely. So, putting the wrong finger on the wrong hand. If you had different colors and you could color their hand... I don't know. Somehow I see that as a huge problem.

The times of day is useful, I'm guessing at the noon hour is maybe when he was eating, so I'm wondering if he's using his hand more for eating, and as a therapist of might suggests during breakfast, lunch, and dinner at least try to use that affected arm and hand more
comment: identifying task with activity, meal time

chunk_text: There's always talk or I guess hope that sometimes people, especially after a stroke, it would be beneficial if they could come back for what we call, booster shots of therapy. So, six months or a year post-stroke they've finished all their rehab, they've been home for a while, they fall into a routine or a rut, and sometimes that leads to disuse or continued learned nonuse of their affected arm and hand. And, it would be nice if they could come back periodically, even for short periods of time, to maybe have a few sessions of Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy, or revisit some exercises or activities. So, that would be a way I could envision using the months or the years. I'm not sure you would have a client be compliant enough to wear the sensors that frequently every single day of the year.
comment: using longitudinal data when back to therapy

chunk_text: Time was limited. So, you've got a full caseload if you're seeing 10 patients in a day, that takes up pretty much all of your day, not including documentation time and if you have to do any administrative things. So, there's really not time.

typically the assessments would be administered the first time you meet the client, and then for periodic follow-up assessments, usually when the third party payer require a justification of more therapy, and that varied depending on the third party payer. Sometimes it was every other week, sometimes it was once a month, sometimes it might be after if the physician prescribed X number of treatment sessions, it was after that many treatment sessions we'd have to reevaluate with assessments to get more, if probably they needed more sessions.

Unfortunately, that's usually dictated by how long the third party payers are willing to reimburse for therapy, and the time is decreasing all the time. So, I would say now at an outpatient clinic, probably average two to four weeks, from start of outpatient before they're discharged. And unfortunately, I would wish we could say, we discharge them when we see a plateau in their progress, or they've met all their goal, ideally, when they've met all their goals, then we would discharge. But, it tends to be more reliant on third party payer reimbursement. And so, you know that they're only going to pay for so many visits or such a length of time, so you try to put as much as you can in that time.
Because unfortunately, they're having less and less time spent with us in therapy, so we need to have things that they can improve upon on their own.
When I worked in a clinical setting, probably averaged 10 patients a day, eight to 10 patients a day.

when you ask clients, what did they do with their arm and hand, how much did they use it, the reliability of their reports is so variable. So, having something quantitative like this would be great to have.

But, I also think explaining it to caregivers, and family, and so that they understand as well, so that they can encourage the person. Even though the person is not cognitively intact, they can maybe encourage saying, "Hey, Dad, remember to use your right hand to eat with, so that you can increase the numbers on your ring sensors." Or something like that, I think that would be helpful.
comment: share with caregivers, caregivers can motivate patients

chunk_text: But, it just varies greatly because some patients, they may just need one or two visits maybe for a splint, and then checking the splint and they're done. Other patients you might see for an extended period of time, two to three times a week, some patients you see once a week, so it can just vary greatly.

definitely first thing would be assessing their motor status recovery, evaluating their adherence would be done in all the follow-up visits after the first one. Certainly, in the first visit and subsequent visits identify their goals that they want to achieve, administer the rehab, and yes, prescribe home exercise.

A duration and B duration better than the first one. Like I said, I think hand dominance plays into how much you're using the affected limb, depending on what's your dominant side. That's why I think A duration and B duration give us a more meaningful data.
comment: A-, B- duration is better than ratio (when consider hand dominance)

chunk_text: I like the idea of having a goal and then reaching that goal. I'm reminded of my Fitbit, whenever I hit 10,000 steps, I get that disco ball thing, it's like yay, 10,000 steps. So something like that where maybe they've reached that goal for the day or that hour, and they get a feedback and that will help. They'll probably want to do that again, hitting that step marker. And it might just encourage them to keep doing that. And I think for some patients that would be helpful. And then some patients, like I said, they might also be motivated by seeing where they started and then where they've progressed to after therapy or after carrying over the concepts of therapy into their outside time.
comment: motivate with goal achievement and progress

chunk_text: I think it depends what the purpose is. If you're tracking overall usage, I think maybe in the beginning, starting with the longer term, looking at maybe a week, one day, one full day and then maybe a week long. And then from data, noticing the trends, if maybe for instance, in the morning for that patient, they're not using their arm as much, really talking to them and figuring out what are the tasks that they're doing, how are they doing it, what do those movement patterns look like, can we simulate what he's doing? And then talking about how we can encourage them to use the arm more functionally and also in a good movement pattern during those tasks. He could also be resting too. So it would be during active daytime. So I think it would just be during the day and when he's active and engaging ADLs or IDLs. Obviously not when he's watching TV or just lounging around or sleeping. Meaningful data. So I think starting with the week long or several days up to a week long and then seeing the trends from there and then breaking it down, like I said.
comment: start with long term to see trend and break down

chunk_text: I think it might be helpful for the patients, for those who are very interested in their own recovery to see this in the data, but ae bit more patient friendly because I think the graphs might be a bit much for them. It's a feedback for them. Like "Oh, I did this much with both my arms or I did this much with my affected arm." That's a good thing.

I think it should probably be simplified for patients, especially since some patients after stroke, they might have some cognitive deficits too, as a result of stroke, so making it as simple as possible. Maybe colors or some marker.
comment: cognitive impaired patients hard to understand, maybe coloring, marker, simplified version would be helpful

chunk_text: I think it's helpful, it helps to contribute to the full picture, but I don't think on his own it's enough to be a standalone assessment.

I think it's very difficult. I think you have to take their word, take their reports, their self verbal report, or a family member can provide some input on how they're doing on ADL or on compliance with whatever you're prescribing on home exercise program, or on wearing a splint. It is difficult.

I think we would look at it together maybe at a progress note session when we reevaluate. And then we talk about how things have been going, if things are getting better. And I think we would bring it up. I don't think it would be like, "Oh, I noticed you didn't use your arm at 11:00 in the morning. What were you doing?" More like, "Let's look at this together. What do you notice? Is there enough?" And talk about what activities they're doing. Yeah. Like I said before, collaborate on how to incorporate the affected limb more in their day-to-day activity.
comment: interview with patients, identify home task

chunk_text: I think you can kind of figure it out, just based on the amount of progress someone makes.

I think you're implying maybe insurance or the payers. As a [Hospital], luckily we're not as closely bound to the payer source. We can provide a lot of equipment and we can practice, not within the restrictions of insurance as a payer source. But I will say that for the FIM, which we use inpatient a lot, it's the most common one, that we do have a database that is kept of all of the inpatient, acute rehab patients who come through. That is being kept track of by CARF. CARF accreditations is a committee that accredits different rehab programs for a standard of care. They do track that as an outcome measure. But in terms of other assessments, like range of motion or the strength testing or coordination, those are, I guess, for us and also for the patient. It's best practice to use these objective measures, so we try to incorporate it, but there isn't really a third person or third party, like an insurance payer, in my setting, that really affects the use of these tools.

I would be curious about his routine. It seems around 12 or lunchtime, he was using his affected side a little bit more. That's the task where maybe he was preparing lunch and he needed to use both arms or use the affected arm more in a five minute task.

After they go home, they might get some home services, home PT, home OT, where they get settled in their home, and the therapist makes sure that they're able to manage safely at home. They review transfers, bathroom transfers, mobility, stairs, things like that. And then, after they go through home therapy, then they will go to outpatient. So, by the time they get to outpatient, it could be months. It could be two to three months, or even longer, after their stroke.

I would be curious about how he performed the task. I don't think we can completely rely on the sensor data. If I were to see this and hear him say that he doesn't use his arm while driving, I would have him show me what he does with the wheel in a car. I think that's 1 of the things that OTs do is they do a task analysis. They observe how someone performs an activity and then breaks down the task into different components to really assess and to make changes as necessary to how someone performs an activity.
I would be interested in seeing the data, definitely, because, like you said, outside of therapy sessions, we don't constantly monitor how much they carry over those concepts, neuro rehab

I would be interested in that as a component of the overall picture. Knowing how much someone using an arm and referencing that to their self support. Then also to the objective measures to see if that's consistent with their progress or their lack of progress. I think that would be helpful to put together full clinical picture.
comment: objective measure to see overall picture

chunk_text: I would say I wouldn't be assessing them every few days or even every week. I think so assessing a function using the FIM levels, we would be assessing that pretty much every time we do an ADL. Every time we work with someone we're assessing that and providing a FIM level during our treatment session. But if it's something like checking their range of motion or their strength, then it might be like at the progress note, so two to three weeks.

ideally if someone's in that stage where they have some movement and they're capable of using the arm and just not motivated or for whatever reason aren't engaging or carrying it over outside of sessions, I think this would be a great tool to monitor that and then also review with the patient.
If there are other categories more specific, like when he's at home, what is he doing exactly?

If you're really thorough, you can give them a sheet of paper with a log, and maybe that's something that you... that I've done in the past. I've done that for inpatients, just to see how compliant someone is following a wearing schedule for a splint, for instance. And, I had the nurses check off or write the times that they wore something, the splints, for. So, those are strategies that I've done, either self report from the patient or caregiver, caregiver report, and using a paper log.

In inpatient and acute care we usually just assess ADLs. Based on FIM levels, functional independence measure, seeing how much assistance they need for different tasks, like upper body dressing, lower body dressing, grooming, that's a pretty standard functional assessment for acute care, and also for inpatient rehab. In acute care, we also use a lot of cognitive assessments, but what's more relevant to your study and your settings that they're looking at and specifically to the stroke population, we would use manual muscle test, range of motion, using the goniometer. Those are very basic. More specific to stroke, we would just test grasp and grip strength. We would also use dynamometer or pinch to asses exact force that the patient has in their grasp. Other measures are the ARAT, the Action Research Arm Test. We use that instead of the Fugl-Meyer, which is the common one for stroke. Other ones that we use, that we have, the Jebsen, I think. I know the nine hole peg one is standardized and is a good one for stroke, but we don't have that one, so we don't use that one. Mostly FIM. FIM is pretty standard, range of motion, manual muscle test dynamometer or grip/grasp strength testing. Depending on the deficit, we would also use specific ones for assessing fine motor.

In the acute care setting, in the hospital setting, we mostly evaluate patients, assess them for their ADLs, assess cognition or any other areas of functional performance that the medical team is concerned about for discharge planning purposes primarily. But if the OT is on the inpatient rehab service rotation, then it's mostly treatment and those sessions would be an hour for each patient. A little bit more intensive and they're treatment based as opposed to evaluation.
In the inpatient setting, it's very easy to make these goals based on the FIM levels, because you assess those at evaluation, and then you assess them every time you do an ADL with them.

All OT is ideally functional. But I think patients, I feel like their scope changes a little bit because ideally when they improve their range of motion or strength or decrease specificity, that will help their function in ADL. And, I think therapists try to make those goals as functional as possible. But, when someone's outpatient, they're not necessarily getting dressed in that clinic. They're not working with the therapist on all of those ADLs as they would be very heavily in inpatient rehab. So outpatient, it can progress to ADLs and more IADLs, even. Ultimately, the focus will be on function and hopefully ADLs. It will improve ADLs, but I don't think it's necessarily the case.

iPad would be the best because you would be able to move that around easily. Sometimes I even just print. It's bad, but I print the data from my computer before the session, and then I just bring it to the patient and then we show them. Or I print things out for patients all the time. So that's what I would do. Something that's printer-friendly or probably iPad-friendly because those are the most. That's what I would [crosstalk]
comment: iPad or print out for sharing sensor data

it seems like when he was driving, he was using the affected side a little bit more than when he was at home, just looking at where the blue bars land in comparison to the ones where he was at home. So, maybe his driving is, ideally, a bimanual task where you're using both hands. That makes me feel a little bit better that he's on the road and he's using the affected arm as well. I think I would be curious what he was doing at home, so 10 and 11...

chunk_text: It tells me some information, but I think overall, I wouldn't take this standalone for a patient's compliance. They wouldn't take it alone for the whole picture. So I think movement pattern and like a video would be even more helpful for a therapist. Seeing how people do certain things but I think for a basic wearable sensor, it's to help to see overall what that level of usage is.

chunk_text: It would show the range, it would show the periods when they're not using their arm as much or periods when they are actively engaging. There are certain tasks that you have to use both hands, for example, washing your hands that's bilateral, you have to do that or else your hand are not going to get clean. Whereas you can do one hand activity like brushing your teeth although you do need to open up that cap. So we think about that a lot when we treat as therapists and knowing which tasks to introduce during therapy sessions, in order to promote bilateral use or to modify to single use, if the patient really can't even engage that periodically or midline.

maybe we would look at the graph at the patient level, in a patient-friendly setting. And then probably look at that one day period at the beginning and then towards the end, before the patient gets discharged from therapy. That's the focus of OT. He wants to use his arm more. So we would use this in the beginning, see where that baseline is. And then towards the end, after we've talked about different strategies and done a lot of repetitions and worked through how to move that arm, and after doing all the other therapies that we would, then at the end right before discharge, we would look at the data again together and look at the progress.

chunk_text: Not everyone is a good patient for this. They have to have the right level of limb motor function. chunk_text: So at that point, like I said, then the focus is a little less on ADL, because you've already gone through inpatient rehab. Potentially, you've gone through home PT and OT. And, by the time you get to outpatient, that's a few months out of a way from your stroke. And by then... There's still room for potentially regaining motor function at that point. But, at that point, it's really addressing what the residual deficits are, and improving those will theoretically, right, help improve your function in ADL. The ADL, some of them have already been figured out in the first stages of rehab.

It's almost like a computer screen, a big TV screen. And we do a lot of different activities on it. If you look that up, it's a pretty common thing that therapists use for different neuro patients. And the way they break it up, the performance results, someone can see their accuracy for hitting different targets on the board. They can see their speed, their reaction time, things like that. And some patients are very interested in that. Others don't really care, but I think you just have to what works for different patients, what they're motivated by. And I think, especially now with so many people having a Fitbit, they know what that is like, hitting that marker, getting that positive feedback.
comment: using big screen to share data to patients, motivate patients

chunk_text: Any kind of therapy they receive or they do with an OT in that setting, it's more focused on that bonus, gaining more range of motion, getting more from their hands, improving coordination, preventing further spasticity, things like that. ultimately my goal is for them to complete the activity, to be independent in an ADL, whether that means using the affected limb or using a combination of compensatory and using the affected limb.

We set goals too. Usually inpatient, we don't set goals for gaining strength or gaining range of motion necessarily. We make goals very function based as OTs. I usually like to check it in the middle and then at time of discharge, just to see what the progress is over an extended amount of time.

we try to make those goals, so that they're realistic and be able to achieve. They're not all necessarily goals. Say, that the patient will be independent with something, because maybe that's not their goal, maybe they have family who's able to help fill in that hole. And so, we just inch that gap, so that the patient can move on to the next level, where the patient hopefully can go home from acute rehab. Or, maybe they need a little bit time and they may have to go to a subacute to keep working on therapy before they ultimately go to a home or another kind of supported setting, if they don't have support.

when they're in inpatient rehab, we only see them for an hour of therapy for a day, and then the rest of the time they're not monitored by therapist directly. They're not receiving therapy. The time that we have with them is pretty limited, and then outside of that, they're on their own.
comment: Can't be monitored outside therapy sessions even among inpatients

for outpatient setting it would be mostly treatments as well.
